clinical_history: Elderly female with a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation who presented with CHF exacerbation and a recent history of syncope. The following ECG is most consistent with which ONE of the following diagnoses?
diagnosis: This ECG shows underlying sinus rhythm (of note, there is right/ left arm lead reversal!) with prominent Q-T/U prolongation and bursts of polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, rate about 190 bpm, diagnostic of non-sustained torsades de pointes. This life-threatening arrhythmia may be the result of a wide variety of drugs, electrolyte disturbances, especially hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia, high degree AV heart blocks, as well as hereditary long QT syndromes ("channelopathies"). In some cases, a combination of factors may contribute.  This woman was on dofetilide therapy (a class III drug) for treatment of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. An echocardiogram revealed mildly-moderately depressed left ventricular ejection fraction of with mid to distal septal and inferior/lateral hypokinesis. Note the QS complexes in V1-V2. Torsades de pointes here was  induced by the dofetilide, in the setting of underlying coronary heart disease and reduced renal clearance.
